trigger: when calling functions that return errors
body: Always check returned errors immediately. Never use _ to discard errors unless you've explicitly decided it's safe and documented why.

trigger: when opening files, connections, or acquiring locks
body: Use defer immediately after acquiring a resource for cleanup. Place defer right after the error check for the acquisition.
Define interfaces at the consumer side, not the producer side. Keep interfaces small (1-3 methods). Accept interfaces, return structs.
Use table-driven tests with subtests (t.Run) for functions with multiple input/output cases. Name test cases descriptively.

trigger: when writing HTTP handlers or long-running operations
body: Accept context.Context as the first parameter. Propagate it to all downstream calls. Use it for cancellation and timeouts.
Always ensure goroutines have a way to exit (context cancellation, done channel, or WaitGroup). Never fire-and-forget goroutines without cleanup.
Design structs so their zero value is useful. Use pointer fields only when nil is a meaningful distinct state from zero value.
Run 'go mod tidy' after adding or removing imports to keep go.mod and go.sum clean.
